1: the person remembers that a family should be created, but waits for the partner to take the initiative and is impatient in relationships. The person remembers that a family should be built, yet makes no particular effort in that direction. They wait for circumstances to bring everything to them on their own. Low initiative. “I want to, but I don’t act.”
2: the person remembers that a family should be created, but waits for the partner to take the initiative and is impatient in relationships. The person remembers that a family should be built, yet makes no particular effort in that direction. They wait for circumstances to bring everything to them on their own. Low initiative. “I want to, but I don’t act.”
3: the person wavers between creating a family and not wanting to do so at all. If work captures their interest, they can devote all their time entirely to it. They are caught between two states: torn between building a family and the desire not to do so at all. Because of this — not knowing what they themselves want — if they are not ready, they may push the partner away or leave the relationship themselves. Children of those with Family 3 may feel jealous of their parents toward their brothers and sisters.
4:
a strong quality of a family person; they cannot live without a family. Yet they are quite demanding of their partner.
They rarely initiate a divorce and strive with all their might to preserve the family and the relationship. The most harmonious coefficient. The program of “building relationships,” the urge to be part of a group/family/society. An understanding of family values — We instead of I, an obsessive preoccupation with forming a Couple. The “marriage instinct.”
5:
a strong quality of a family person; they cannot live without a family. Yet they are quite demanding of their partner.
They rarely initiate a divorce and strive with all their might to preserve the family and the relationship. The most harmonious coefficient. The program of “building relationships,” the urge to be part of a group/family/society. An understanding of family values — We instead of I, an obsessive preoccupation with forming a Couple. The “marriage instinct.”
6: the person so overrates the partner and idealizes the relationship that they are ready to give it up altogether. They get what they want — all or nothing; flexibility is essential. High demands. They spend a very long time choosing Their ideal, which holds them back from committing to a relationship. A perfectionist in everything.

They spend a very long time choosing Their ideal, which holds them back from committing to a relationship. A perfectionist in everything.
